title: Dog Foaming at the Mouth and Trouble Breathing: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog is foaming at the mouth and struggling to breathe, this is a high-priority emergency. Seek immediate veterinary care now.
content_html:
TL;DR: Foaming at the mouth and respiratory distress is a life-threatening emergency that requires immediate veterinary care to prevent organ failure. If your dog is gasping or has blue-tinged gums, transport them to an emergency clinic right away.

What causes my dog to experience foaming at the mouth and respiratory distress?

Foaming at the mouth combined with respiratory distress (dyspnea) occurs when a dog is unable to clear fluids or saliva from their airway, or when they are gasping so heavily that saliva aerates into a foam. This can be caused by various underlying issues including congestive heart failure, toxin ingestion, heatstroke, or severe allergic reactions.

Is foaming at the mouth and respiratory distress a medical emergency for my dog?

  • Yes, this is a High Urgency situation. Respiratory distress is life-threatening because it indicates your dog is not receiving enough oxygen to their vital organs.
  • Look for critical warning signs: Blue or purple-tinged gums, extreme lethargy, or heavy gasping.
  • Take immediate action: You must transport your dog to the nearest emergency veterinary clinic immediately if these symptoms occur.

How can taking a photo or video help the veterinarian triage my dog's condition?

  • Capture the symptoms: If you can do so safely without delaying your transit, take a quick photo or five-second video of the breathing effort and foam.
  • Identify the foam type: Seeing the color of the foam (white, pink, or blood-tinged) provides critical diagnostic clues.
  • Observe muscle movement: Veterinarians use the visual of specific chest muscle efforts to differentiate between lung issues and upper airway obstructions before starting a physical exam.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Compromised breathing in dogs manifests with an increased respiratory rate and effort, followed by a change in the respiratory pattern and postural changes such as standing with elbows abducted and the back arched or high on the rear haunches with the head and neck extended. Labored, open-mouth breathing and changes in mucous membrane color (gray or blue, indicating cyanosis) develop last and indicate significant loss of pulmonary function and impending pulmonary arrest. If the animal is cyanotic and collapsed, especially if pawing at the face, an upper airway obstruction may be present, requiring a Heimlich maneuver or chest compression to relieve the obstruction and manual airway clearing. In unconscious and non-breathing animals, mouth-to-nose resuscitation should be performed at a rate of 10 breaths/min after 3-4 initial strong breaths. Oxygen therapy should be implemented, and stress and anxiety should be minimized, potentially with acepromazine (0.03 mg/kg SC), unless the animal is depressed, hypotensive, or hypothermic, or opiates (e.g., methadone, 0.3-0.5 mg/kg, SC, IM) can be administered.

Chapter: Emergency, Respiratory

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1663)

faqs.question: Could my dog have rabies if they are foaming at the mouth?
faqs.answer: While foaming is a classic symptom of rabies, it is much more commonly caused by seizures, heatstroke, or oral irritation in modern pets. However, any foaming should be treated as a medical emergency.

faqs.question: Should I try to clear my dog's mouth?
faqs.answer: No. If your dog is struggling to breathe, putting your fingers in their mouth puts you at risk of being bitten and may further distress the dog, worsening their oxygen intake.

faqs.question: What if the foam is pink or red?
faqs.answer: Pink or frothy red foam often indicates the presence of blood or fluid in the lungs (pulmonary edema), which requires immediate oxygen therapy and stabilization by a veterinarian.
